summary: Gruber proposes adding a small trackpad to the iPad Smart Keyboard, not for a system-wide mouse cursor, but primarily for text editing and Apple TV-style navigation. He argues that the current situation is backwards: the on-screen keyboard offers decent cursor movement but poor typing, while hardware keyboards offer decent typing but terrible cursor movement. He draws on iOS's existing two-finger trackpad mode and tvOS's UIFocusEngine as precedents showing cursor-free trackpad interaction is already viable. He acknowledges physical design constraints and addresses the objection that a trackpad would create mouse-cursor expectations, arguing users would adapt quickly. He also critiques an alternative proposal to make the keyboard surface itself touch-sensitive, finding a discrete trackpad simpler and more practical.
key_points: The iPad has an inverted problem: on-screen keyboards enable good cursor movement but bad typing, while hardware keyboards enable good typing but bad cursor movement
tvOS's UIFocusEngine already proves trackpads can work without mouse cursors — moving selection rather than a pointer
The concern that a trackpad would create mouse-cursor expectations is overblown; users who chose an iPad over a MacBook will adapt
Modes in UI design should be avoided when possible — a discrete trackpad that's always a trackpad is better than a keyboard that sometimes becomes one
The biggest practical obstacle is the Smart Keyboard's folding cover design, which leaves no physical room for a trackpad
money_quotes: I'm not talking about adding an on-screen mouse cursor to iOS for clicking and dragging. That's a terrible idea.
It seems like a regression to 1983 to encourage an entirely keyboard-based routine for text editing.
The original Mac didn't even have arrow keys on the keyboard, to force users to use the mouse for moving the insertion point.
When you're using the Smart Keyboard you have a decent keyboard for typing but no good way to move the insertion point or select text.
Using your finger to touch the screen is imprecise, and, when an iPad is propped up laptop-style, ergonomically undesirable.
If they wanted a MacBook they'd be using a MacBook. By definition anyone using an iPad is not expecting it to act like a Mac.
It's almost always better to avoid modality if you can. With a discrete trackpad, the keyboard is always the keyboard and the trackpad is always the trackpad.
I say don't overthink it. Just put a small trackpad under the keyboard for moving the insertion point around and Apple TV-style navigation. Simple.
key_insight: The iPad's text editing problem is an ergonomic inversion that a small, dedicated trackpad could solve without importing the Mac's mouse-cursor paradigm, using tvOS's focus-based navigation as the model.
